fair_information.theme_if_applicable: Homegrown Creativity: From Our Barns to Our Backyards

category_3___new_display_method_andor_pr.why_was_this_one_method_of_display_and_o:
Our Competitive Exhibits team created this new display method and prop—a modular, barn-themed vertical shelving system with integrated LED lighting and rotating story panels—because our existing tabletop displays for home arts and youth crafts had become overcrowded, visually flat, and difficult for guests to navigate. Over the past five years our participation numbers in these departments have grown by roughly 25–30%, especially in youth arts and photography, but our physical space and old-style banquet tables with draped cloths remained the same. This resulted in entries being lined up shoulder-to-shoulder, with lower-scoring pieces sometimes hidden behind ribbons or signage, and very little opportunity to tell the story of the exhibitors or the fair’s theme.

During our 2019 post-fair survey, we received repeated comments that the home arts building was “cluttered,” “hard to see,” and “felt like a storage room instead of a showcase.” Several parents also mentioned that their children’s projects were displayed too low or too high for grandparents and younger siblings to comfortably view. Our superintendents and volunteers echoed these concerns, noting that judging days were chaotic because entries had to be shuffled and stacked to fit the available space. One judge even mentioned needing to move a quilt three times just to properly evaluate it, which raised concerns about potential damage.

We chose a barn-inspired vertical display method specifically to align with our 2021 fair theme, “Homegrown Creativity: From Our Barns to Our Backyards,” and to celebrate the region’s agricultural roots while still feeling updated and modern. The new prop units are shaped like stylized barn walls with faux timber framing, metal roof accents, and neutral, matte backing boards. This design helps entries stand out without clashing with the diverse colors and textures of quilts, paintings, and craft items. The integrated LED strip lighting along the top and sides was chosen to evenly illuminate entries, reduce harsh shadows, and make fine details—such as brush strokes, stitching, and pencil shading—easier to see and judge.

Another major reason for this new method was accessibility and safety. Our previous setup often required step stools to hang items higher on pegboards, which was not ideal for older volunteers. The new units are designed with standardized hanging rails and adjustable shelves that can be repositioned at safe, reachable heights. This allows us to place youth entries at a child’s eye level, while adult and senior entries are grouped at typical standing eye height. The backs of the units are solid and weighted, reducing the risk of tipping if a guest leans in closely.

We also wanted a more educational and story-driven experience for visitors. Each display section includes a rotating story panel—a magnetic, chalkboard-style sign where we highlight a featured exhibitor each day, sharing their name, age (if youth), town, and a short narrative about their project and how long they have been participating in the fair. This method encourages guests to slow down, read, and connect personally with the exhibitors rather than just glancing at ribbons. It also gives us flexibility to feature different communities within our region, including first-time exhibitors, multi-generation families, and 4-H or FFA clubs.

From an operational standpoint, the modular design allows us to reconfigure the layout each year depending on entry numbers. The units are built on lockable caster bases so they can be rolled into various formations—straight rows, zig-zag patterns, or small alcoves—allowing better crowd flow and the ability to create themed zones (e.g., “Quilts & Textiles,” “Youth Mixed Media,” “Photography Corner”). This was especially important in 2021 as we were still mindful of COVID-related crowd spacing and wanted to avoid tight bottlenecks.

Finally, cost and durability were key considerations in choosing this method and prop. We designed the units so they could be constructed largely in-house by our maintenance crew using common lumber sizes, off-the-shelf lighting components, and standard hardware. The intention is that these props will last at least 8–10 years with only minor touch-ups (paint, replacement LEDs), making the up-front investment worthwhile. In short, this method and prop were created to solve overcrowding, improve visibility and accessibility, reinforce our fair theme, and elevate the overall visitor and exhibitor experience in a way that is flexible and sustainable for future fairs.
category_3___new_display_method_andor_pr.on_what_class_or_division_of_entries_was:
The new barn-themed modular display method and prop were used primarily in the Home Arts & Creative Crafts Department, with a special focus on the following classes and divisions:

• Adult Fine Arts (Division A): Oil and acrylic painting, watercolor, drawing/illustration, mixed media, and small framed works up to 24" x 36". These pieces were hung on the vertical barn wall sections using adjustable rail hooks, allowing us to create salon-style groupings without overcrowding.

• Youth Art (Division B, ages 8–12) and Teen Art (Division C, ages 13–18): School art projects, 4-H art entries, and independent youth artworks including posters, colored pencil drawings, and small canvases. For these classes, the shelves and hanging rails were lowered to youth eye level, and we clustered entries by age group so families could easily locate their child’s work.

• Photography (Division D – Amateur and Division E – Youth): 5"x7", 8"x10", and 11"x14" mounted prints and framed photographs. The vertical nature of the display allowed us to separate categories such as “Farm Life,” “People/Portraits,” and “Scenic North Dakota” with clear labeling on the barn-style crossbeam headers.

• Needlework & Textiles (Division F): Smaller quilted wall hangings, embroidery samplers, cross-stitch pieces, and decorative textile art that could safely be hung without excessive weight. Larger bed-sized quilts were still displayed on traditional quilt racks and poles, but smaller items were integrated into the barn units to visually tie the department together.

• Youth Crafts (Division G): 3-D school craft projects, recycled art, Lego and building block creations (up to a defined size), and decorated household items. These were placed on the adjustable shelves within each unit, with clear acrylic risers used in some cases to create multi-level arrangements so that no project was hidden behind another.

• Special Themed Class (Division H – “Homegrown Creativity”): Entries specifically created to reflect the 2021 fair theme, including barn and farm-inspired crafts, paintings of local landscapes, and photography featuring home gardens and backyard livestock. For this division, we reserved a dedicated cluster of three barn units near the entrance to the building, using the story panels to spotlight exhibitors who had strong personal connections to agriculture or multi-generation farm backgrounds.

In total, the new display method and prop were used for approximately 420 entries across these classes and divisions. They were grouped in three main pods of units: one pod for adult fine arts and photography, one for youth and teen art, and one for youth crafts and theme entries. The barn aesthetic and flexible configuration allowed each class to retain its unique identity while still feeling like part of a cohesive, curated exhibition space.
category_3___new_display_method_andor_pr.height: Each individual barn-wall display unit measures approximately 7 feet 6 inches (7'6") in overall height, including the decorative peaked “barn roof” header. The flat vertical display surface beneath the header is about 6 feet 8 inches tall, providing ample hanging space for art and photography while keeping the top row of entries within a reasonable viewing and mounting height for staff and visitors.
category_3___new_display_method_andor_pr.width: Each display unit is 4 feet (48 inches) wide from outer frame edge to outer frame edge. Units are designed to be used side by side or back to back, so a typical run of three units in a row spans roughly 12 feet of linear wall space, not including small gaps for access and safety clearances.
category_3___new_display_method_andor_pr.depth: The base of each unit, including the support feet and lockable casters, measures approximately 2 feet (24 inches) deep from front edge to back edge. The framed display wall itself is about 4 inches deep, with the remainder of the depth accounted for by the weighted base and a slight backward angle to improve stability and viewing comfort.
category_3___new_display_method_andor_pr.how_much_did_this_cost_to_create_cost_of: The total cost to design and construct six barn-wall display units was approximately $3,450. This includes around $1,980 in materials (lumber, plywood, screws and hardware, paint and stain, LED strip lights and power supplies, caster wheels, metal roof trim accents, acrylic story panels, and hanging hardware) and an estimated $1,470 in in-kind labor provided by our fair maintenance staff and a local volunteer carpenter. Labor was calculated at an internal rate of $21 per hour for roughly 70 hours of combined design, construction, painting, wiring, and installation time. While the labor was not an out-of-pocket cash expense due to being completed by existing staff and volunteers, we tracked it for budgeting and future grant reporting purposes.
